content: The Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) states that asset prices fully reflect all available information, making it impossible to consistently beat the market. Three forms: weak (prices reflect historical data—technical analysis fails), semi-strong (prices reflect all public information—fundamental analysis fails), and strong (prices reflect even insider information). EMH implies passive indexing is optimal since active management can't add value after costs. Evidence is mixed: anomalies like momentum, value premium, and post-earnings announcement drift challenge EMH. Behavioral finance attributes anomalies to psychological biases. Most practitioners accept markets are mostly efficient but not perfectly so, leaving room for limited active management.
